Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd try to set aside time, but I'm the kind of person who learns best by doing things with my hands, so I'd often end up working through problems physically or making models rather than sitting at a screen. I'd have bursts of intense studying, maybe 23 hours some weeks, but it wasn't evenly spread out. I'd often study late at night when my anxiety was keeping me awake anyway.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on what was due. I wasn't someone who logged in at the same time every day or anything like that. When deadlines loomed, I'd suddenly become very focused, but otherwise I'd drift. Life stuff would pull me away—I'm 24 and juggling more than just school, you know? My attendance was pretty low because I found I could learn the material better on my own terms.
Q3: Usually what got me to log in was checking if there was something due soon or if grades had been posted. The anxiety of potentially missing something important was a big motivator, honestly. Sometimes I'd get a notification and feel this surge of panic, so I'd log in just to calm my nerves. Other times it was to grab materials I needed for my hands-on study sessions offline.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in became more automatic, especially mid-semester when assignments were coming regularly. I'd wake up, check my phone, and the LMS would be one of my first stops. But that habit would break whenever I had a stretch without deadlines. It never really stuck as a permanent routine for me—more like waves of engagement.
Q5: The downloadable resources and practice problems were gold for me. As a kinesthetic learner, I needed things I could print out, mark up, and physically work through. Video content was okay but I'd only watch about 15 minutes before getting restless. Discussion posts helped when I could see how other students approached problems—that peer interaction gave me different angles to think about.
Q6: I mostly ignored the longer video lectures and some of the supplementary readings. Sitting and watching passively just doesn't work for my brain. I'd skim discussion forums sometimes without really engaging deeply. If something felt theoretical without a clear application, I'd put it off indefinitely. Time felt too precious to spend on things that didn't directly help me understand through doing.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or application. If I could work through a problem and physically see my progress, that was engaging. Things that connected to real-world examples or let me build something concrete were valuable. Abstract theory dumps without context? Those felt like a waste, even if they were supposedly important.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford mentally. With my anxiety levels, I was always calculating what absolutely needed to get done versus what was extra. Required activities had this weight to them that made me push through even when I didn't want to. Optional stuff I'd only touch if I was genuinely curious or if it seemed like it would directly boost my understanding for an exam.
Q9: Oh, absolutely. Most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs, print worksheets, and work through problems with pen and paper spread across my desk. I had a whiteboard where I'd map out concepts physically. I also used YouTube tutorials and external websites that explained things in more hands-on ways. The LMS was more like a starting point than where actual learning happened.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be up working through problems from printed materials, making flashcards, or building study aids—none of which showed up anywhere in the system. I'd study with classmates in person, working through concepts together. Sometimes I'd spend hours on a single difficult concept using external resources. The LMS would just show I hadn't logged in.
Q11: All the time. I'd download everything I needed in one session and then not log back in for days while I worked through it all offline. It was more efficient for me to have everything locally where I could access it without internet distractions. One download session could fuel a week of actual studying that the system never saw.
Q12: My anxiety would spike at random times, and during those periods, even looking at the LMS felt overwhelming. There were weeks where life stuff—family obligations, work stress—made it hard to care about school. Sometimes after getting feedback that surprised me, I'd need time to process before I could re-engage. The low points felt isolating even though I knew I could bounce back.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or anxious, I'd avoid the LMS entirely because it represented all these obligations. Competing responsibilities meant I had to triage constantly—sometimes school lost. Confusion about assignment requirements would make me freeze up and procrastinate on logging in. I'd use the LMS less when I was overwhelmed, even though that's probably when I needed it most.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials with glazed eyes, not really absorbing anything. I'd have the LMS open but my mind would be elsewhere, worrying about other things. Sometimes I'd watch videos at 2x speed just to technically complete them without really learning. It was going through the motions without being present.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because they triggered my anxiety about being judged by peers. Even when I knew something was important, if it felt emotionally heavy, I'd push it off. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt more capable. The irony is the postponing made my anxiety worse, but in the moment, avoidance felt like self-protection.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the engine that drove my LMS usage. I'd have low activity for days, then suddenly be very active right before something was due. It wasn't ideal, but it worked for getting assignments completed. The deadline pressure actually helped cut through my anxiety paralysis sometimes—like the fear of missing the deadline outweighed other fears.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Good feedback surprised me and motivated me to engage more. Unexpected criticism would send me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course for a bit while I processed it. The CBT sessions I've been doing helped me handle feedback better, but my initial reaction was always emotional. I'd eventually use feedback constructively, but it took time.
Q18: The course structure with clear deadlines helped me because I need external accountability. But the heavy online component was challenging for someone who learns by doing. Having multiple online courses meant I was constantly context-switching, which was draining. The structure gave me framework, but it didn't always match how I naturally learn best.
Q19: Downloadable resources made engagement easier because I could take them offline. Clear rubrics helped my anxiety because I knew exactly what was expected. What made it harder was when participation required real-time engagement or when instructions were vague. Too many discussion requirements felt forced and anxiety-inducing rather than genuinely collaborative.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for understanding without finding it—clicking around trying to make sense of things but not producing work because they're stuck. Or they could be anxious like me, checking and rechecking things without actually submitting. High activity doesn't mean productive activity; sometimes it's just anxiety-driven clicking.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. My attendance and login frequency were low, but I still got a distinction because my actual learning happened offline. I completed assignments at a high rate and did well on exams because I found ways to learn that worked for me, even if they weren't visible. The system doesn't capture studying that happens away from the screen.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone's active, what they click on, completion rates. That's useful surface data. But it completely misses the quality of engagement, offline studying, emotional state, and whether someone actually understood anything. It's like measuring how long someone sits in a library without knowing if they read a single page.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious and motivated to dig deeper, when I'm making connections and applying concepts. For me, engagement is often physical and emotional—I need to feel involved, not just technically present. True engagement survives even when the LMS is closed.
Q24: The LMS can't show my anxiety levels or how much mental energy it takes me to even open the platform some days. It doesn't capture my offline studying, my peer study sessions, or the hours I spend with printed materials. It can't measure my resilience—how I keep pushing through despite feeling overwhelmed. My engagement is real; it just doesn't always leave digital footprints.
